sections.title: Phrasal verbs
items: **brush up (on)** — practise and improve your skills or knowledge of something
**come (a)round (to)** — change your opinion or decision because someone has persuaded you to agree with them
**come up with** — think of something such as an idea or a plan
**face up to** — accept something and try to deal with it
**figure out** — be able to understand something or solve a problem; understand what someone is like
**hit upon** — suddenly have an idea; discover something by chance
**make out** — see, hear or understand someone or something with difficulty; suggest, imply
**mull over** — think carefully about something over a period of time
**piece together** — learn the truth about something by considering all the separate bits of information
**puzzle out** — solve a confusing or complicated problem by thinking carefully about it
**read up (on/about)** — get information on a particular subject by reading a lot about it
**swot up (on)** — study something very hard, especially for an examination
**take in** — understand and remember something; accept something as real or true; trick someone
**think over** — consider a problem or decision carefully
**think through** — consider the facts about something in an organised and thorough way
**think up** — invent or imagine something, especially an excuse

sections.title: conclusion
items: come to / arrive at / reach a conclusion

sections.title: perspective
items: from another / a different / sb's / etc perspective

sections.title: principle
items: stand by / stick to your principles
a matter / an issue of principle

sections.title: question
items: some question over / as to / about

sections.title: Idioms
items: **go to your head** — if success goes to your head, it makes you think that you are better or more important than you really are
**have your wits about you** — be able to think quickly and make sensible decisions
**in the dark (about)** — not knowing very much about something, because other people are keeping it secret from you
**know what's what** — know the important facts or the real situation
**not have a leg to stand on** — not have any way of proving that you are right about something
**not see the wood for the trees** — used for saying that someone cannot understand what is important because they are thinking too much about small details
**put two and two together** — guess what is happening or what something means based on what you have seen or heard
**quick/slow on the uptake** — taking a very short/long time to understand or realise something
**split hairs** — argue or worry about very small details or differences that are not important
**take stock (of)** — spend some time thinking about the situation you are in before you decide what to do next

items.sentenceBefore: You're being far too
items.sentenceAfter: about your chances — I'm sure you'll pass the exam.
items.originalSentence: You're being far too **optimistic / pessimistic** about your chances — I'm sure you'll pass the exam.
items.completedSentence: You're being far too pessimistic about your chances — I'm sure you'll pass the exam.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The speaker disagrees with a negative outlook — they say you will pass, so the gap must describe an attitude that is too negative.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "pessimistic" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "optimistic" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.optimistic: "optimistic" points the wrong way — the speaker is rejecting too much hope, not supporting it.

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

optionContrast.optimistic: "optimistic" doesn't fit here → "pessimistic" does, because the speaker is rejecting too much hope, not supporting it.

items.sentenceBefore: The documentary was clearly
items.sentenceAfter: in favour of the company it was supposed to be examining.
items.originalSentence: The documentary was clearly **biased / prejudiced** in favour of the company it was supposed to be examining.
items.completedSentence: The documentary was clearly biased in favour of the company it was supposed to be examining.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Media that unfairly supports one side is biased. Prejudiced usually describes a person's unfair attitude, not a one-sided documentary.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "biased" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "prejudiced" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.prejudiced: "prejudiced" does not describe one-sided media as naturally as "biased" does here.

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

optionContrast.prejudiced: "prejudiced" doesn't fit here → "biased" does, because it does not describe one-sided media as naturally as "biased" does here.

optionContrast.dubious: "dubious" doesn't fit here → "cynical" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("about politicians — I never believe a word they say.").

exercises.passage:
The debate about whether schools should focus more on academic knowledge or on practical life skills continues to divide educators. Critics of the current system argue that the national (1) ...... places too much emphasis on preparing students for standardised tests and too little on developing their ability to (2) ...... information critically. In a typical school week, students spend hours (3) ...... for exams, only to forget most of what they have learned shortly afterwards.

What gets lost, argue the critics, is the development of genuinely (4) ...... thinkers — students who can engage in deep analysis and independent reasoning. Universities frequently report that first-year students struggle with (5) ...... because they have never been taught how to work without constant guidance. exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the text by writing the correct form of each word in capitals, one gap at a time.
exercises.passage:
The …(1)… (CONCEIVE) of critical thinking as a teachable skill has gained widespread acceptance in recent years. The …(2)… (ASSUME) that intelligence is fixed at birth has been largely discredited by modern neuroscience. It is …(3)… (DOUBT) true that learning new things actively changes the physical structure of the brain.

However, many students struggle with the …(4)… (CONFUSE) surrounding different study techniques. Which methods are truly effective, and which produce only the …(5)… (BELIEVE) impression of genuine learning? These are important questions. Students who feel …(6)… (CONVINCE) by a teacher's explanation often leave thinking they have understood, only to discover later that they cannot apply what they have learned.

A …(7)… (DECIDE) learner, by contrast, will commit to a method and persevere long enough to see real results. Such a student is not put off by problems that at first seem …(8)… (EXPLAIN). With time, patience, and a truly …(9)… (IMAGINE) approach to problem-solving, most challenges can be overcome. Ultimately, the most …(10)… (DEFINE) measure of learning is not a test score, but the ability to think and act independently.
